name: Horror
description: Stories designed to evoke fear or dread through threat, the uncanny, or the monstrous, often emphasizing vulnerability and survival.

nodes.label: Horror Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised fear, dread, and a confrontation with threatening forces — a story where the world is not safe, something monstrous or uncanny is present, and survival or sanity is genuinely uncertain.
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill the promise of fear and threat
typical_variants: Supernatural horror
Slasher/survival horror
Cosmic/existential horror
Psychological horror
failure_modes: Claims horror but generates no genuine dread
Threat is introduced but never made to feel dangerous
Fear is replaced by spectacle without stakes
signals_in_text: Opening establishes unease or anomaly before full threat is revealed
Audience or reader feels the world is unsafe from the first pages or scenes

nodes.label: Threat Must Be Present, Escalating, and Genuinely Dangerous
nodes.definition: The horror story must contain a threat that is not merely implied but actively present, that grows more dangerous as the narrative progresses, and that poses real consequences — death, destruction, loss of sanity, or worse — to the characters.
exit_conditions: Threat is demonstrably dangerous and escalates across acts
typical_variants: Threat escalates in proximity to protagonist
Threat escalates in power or reach
Threat escalates in understanding — the more known, the worse the situation
failure_modes: Threat is static — same danger level throughout
Threat harms only minor characters while protagonist remains untouched
Danger is repeatedly announced but never lands
signals_in_text: Each act raises the cost of confronting the threat
Characters suffer real harm or loss as threat advances
Protagonist's options narrow as threat escalates

nodes.label: Protagonist Must Be Vulnerable — Outmatched by the Threat
nodes.definition: The protagonist must be genuinely outmatched by the threat — lacking the power, knowledge, resources, or tools to easily overcome it. Vulnerability is the foundation of horror; if the protagonist can simply fight back and win, dread evaporates.
exit_conditions: Audience believes the protagonist could be harmed, destroyed, or overwhelmed
typical_variants: Physical vulnerability — threat is stronger
Epistemic vulnerability — protagonist doesn't understand the threat
Institutional vulnerability — no one will help or believe them
Psychological vulnerability — protagonist's own mind is compromised
failure_modes: Protagonist is too skilled or powerful to feel endangered
Protagonist has easy access to tools or knowledge that neutralize the threat
Safety nets are always available
signals_in_text: Protagonist fails or is injured in confrontations
Plans and escapes fall apart under pressure
Resources and allies are limited or unreliable

nodes.label: Atmosphere of Dread Must Be Sustained
nodes.definition: Horror requires a pervasive atmosphere of dread — not just isolated jump scares or discrete frightening moments, but a sustained sense that the world is wrong, that something terrible is present, and that safety is an illusion. Dread must infuse the story's tone at all levels.
exit_conditions: Atmosphere of dread is maintained across the majority of the narrative, with moments of false relief serving only to deepen the tension
typical_variants: Slow-burn dread building to eruption
Oppressive claustrophobic atmosphere
Uncanny familiar-turned-threatening
Cosmic insignificance weighing on every scene
failure_modes: Long stretches feel safe and comfortable, breaking the horror contract
Dread exists only in designated scare scenes
Comic relief undercuts fear without purpose
signals_in_text: Environmental details are ominous even in quiet scenes
Characters are watchful, tense, or psychologically worn down
Silence and stillness carry as much threat as action

nodes.label: The Threat Must Have a Logic — Rules Even If Unknown to Characters
nodes.definition: Effective horror threats operate by consistent internal rules, even when those rules are not known to the characters or fully revealed to the audience. The threat must behave coherently — its power, limits, and methods must follow some logic, even if that logic is alien or horrifying. Arbitrary or random threats generate confusion, not dread.
exit_conditions: Threat demonstrates consistent behavior that the audience can sense even if characters cannot articulate it
typical_variants: Supernatural rules (iron, salt, names, invitations)
Predatory logic (stalking, territorial, reactive to fear)
Cosmic rules (indifferent, operating on inhuman scales)
Psychological rules (triggered by guilt, desire, or belief)
failure_modes: Threat behaves randomly with no discernible pattern
Threat is arbitrarily powerful or weak based on plot convenience
Rules are stated but not followed consistently
signals_in_text: Characters attempt to understand threat logic and sometimes succeed partially
Threat's behavior, once observed, creates dread of specific triggers
Rules, even unknown, make the threat feel real rather than arbitrary

nodes.label: Supernatural Horror
nodes.definition: Horror in which the threat originates from beyond the natural world — ghosts, demons, curses, haunted objects, possessing entities, or malevolent spiritual forces. The supernatural threatens the protagonist's understanding of reality as well as their physical safety.
entry_conditions: Threat is explicitly supernatural in origin
exit_conditions: Supernatural nature of threat is established and its rules — even if partial — are operative in the story
typical_variants: Haunted location horror (ghost, poltergeist)
Possession or demonic intrusion
Curse or ritual horror
Folkloric creature horror (vampire, werewolf, entity bound by ancient rules)
failure_modes: Supernatural threat behaves like a mundane physical danger with no uncanny dimension
Supernatural rules are inconsistent or unexplained to the point of arbitrariness
Threat is introduced as supernatural but rationalized away
signals_in_text: Phenomena occur that violate physical laws
Characters must confront the limits of rational explanation
Religious, folkloric, or occult frameworks become relevant

nodes.label: Slasher / Survival Horror
nodes.definition: Horror in which the threat is a human or near-human killer — a predator who pursues and eliminates victims in a physically immediate, visceral way. Survival horror emphasizes the desperate attempt to outlast or escape the threat rather than overcome it.
entry_conditions: Threat is a relentless human or near-human predator
exit_conditions: Threat's physical danger is established and survival becomes the primary goal
typical_variants: Classic slasher (masked killer, group of victims)
Siege horror (threat closes in on a barricaded location)
Predator horror (near-human or augmented killer with superhuman persistence)
Home invasion horror
failure_modes: Threat is too easily defeated — survival is not genuinely at risk
Victims behave with implausible stupidity to enable the threat
Physical danger becomes repetitive without escalation
signals_in_text: Victims are eliminated progressively, reducing the group
Escape routes are blocked or exhausted
Physical confrontation with threat is depicted as extremely dangerous

nodes.label: Cosmic / Existential Horror
nodes.definition: Horror in which the threat is vast, unknowable, and indifferent — forces or entities that operate on scales incomprehensible to human understanding, rendering individual survival meaningless and human knowledge inadequate. The horror is the realization of human insignificance.
entry_conditions: Threat operates on a scale or in a mode that fundamentally exceeds human comprehension
exit_conditions: Protagonist's encounter with the threat erodes certainty, sanity, or the meaning of survival itself
typical_variants: Lovecraftian ancient entity horror
Reality-unraveling horror (fabric of existence threatened)
Deep space or deep time horror
Revelation horror (truth is worse than ignorance)
failure_modes: Cosmic threat is reduced to a manageable, defeatable antagonist
Scale of the threat is claimed but not felt
Protagonist retains full sanity and functionality after encountering the unknowable
signals_in_text: Characters experience breakdown of meaning or sanity, not just fear
Knowledge of the threat makes things worse, not better
Resolution — if it comes — is temporary or pyrrhic

nodes.label: Psychological Horror
nodes.definition: Horror in which the primary threat is internal — the protagonist's own mind becomes the source of danger, through trauma, guilt, delusion, or a reality that cannot be trusted. The line between external threat and internal breakdown is deliberately blurred.
entry_conditions: Threat originates in or is amplified by the protagonist's psychological state
exit_conditions: Protagonist's perception and mental stability are under sustained assault, making the audience question what is real
typical_variants: Unreliable narrator horror (is the threat real or imagined?)
Trauma-manifestation horror (the protagonist's past becomes monstrous)
Gaslighting horror (protagonist's reality is actively manipulated)
Dissociation or identity horror (protagonist becomes the threat)
failure_modes: Psychological dimension is resolved into a purely external threat
Ambiguity about reality is used as a cop-out rather than a source of genuine dread
Protagonist's mental state is a backdrop rather than the engine of the horror
signals_in_text: Protagonist doubts their own perceptions and memories
Reality and hallucination are indistinguishable at key moments
Resolution does not fully restore stability — the damage persists

nodes.label: Isolation Amplifies Threat — Characters Cut Off from Help
nodes.definition: The horror setting or circumstances must isolate the protagonist from external help — physically, socially, or institutionally — so that they cannot simply call for assistance, escape to safety, or be rescued. Isolation is the prerequisite for the threat to operate without interference.
exit_conditions: Help is genuinely unavailable or unreachable throughout the threat's operation
typical_variants: Geographic isolation (remote cabin, island, space station)
Communication isolation (no signal, lines cut, no one listening)
Social isolation (no one believes them, no one will come)
Temporal isolation (the threat operates in gaps or at night when help is unavailable)
failure_modes: Help is one phone call away and the protagonist doesn't call without sufficient reason
Isolation is constructed through implausible character decisions rather than genuine circumstance
Isolation resolves mid-story before the threat is resolved
signals_in_text: Attempts to reach help fail or are blocked
Characters explicitly note they are alone
Setting is described in terms of distance from safety

nodes.label: Normal Spaces Become Uncanny — The Familiar Turns Threatening
nodes.definition: Horror derives much of its power from the corruption of the familiar — homes, schools, towns, and faces that should be safe become threatening. The setting must transform from the recognizable into the uncanny, making the ordinary feel wrong.
entry_conditions: Setting established as initially familiar or safe
exit_conditions: Familiar elements of the setting have been invested with dread through the story's progression
typical_variants: The home as haunt (domestic spaces turn threatening)
The trusted figure who becomes monstrous
The childhood familiar that returns as nightmare
The community or institution that conceals horror beneath normalcy
failure_modes: Setting is unfamiliar from the start and never generates uncanny contrast
Familiar space is corrupted but never felt as familiar — no contrast achieved
Uncanny quality is told rather than rendered through sensory detail
signals_in_text: Protagonist interacts with once-safe spaces with new wariness
Familiar objects or routines take on sinister significance
Readers/viewers experience the dissonance between what should be safe and what is now dangerous

nodes.label: Darkness and Concealment — Threat Operates Where It Cannot Be Fully Seen
nodes.definition: Horror threats gain power from concealment — they operate in darkness, at the edges of perception, or in spaces where they cannot be fully observed or understood. Full visibility of the threat often diminishes fear; what is partially seen or only sensed is more terrifying than what is fully revealed.
exit_conditions: Threat's full form, extent, or nature is deliberately withheld or only partially revealed
typical_variants: Physical darkness (threat strikes from shadow)
Perceptual concealment (threat sensed but not seen)
Cognitive concealment (threat's nature is incomprehensible even when observed)
Strategic concealment (threat hides among the familiar)
failure_modes: Threat is fully and clearly revealed too early, reducing dread
Concealment is used mechanically without generating actual fear
Darkness is a setting feature but does not interact with the threat's operation
signals_in_text: Threat is described obliquely, through sound, shadow, or effect rather than direct depiction
Characters' attempts to see or understand the threat are frustrated
Partial revelation increases rather than resolves fear

nodes.label: First Anomaly / Scare Scene — Something Is Wrong
nodes.definition: An early scene in which the first clear signal that something is wrong is established — an anomaly, an unexplained event, a wrong detail that cannot be rationalized away. This scene activates the horror contract with the audience and begins the accumulation of dread.
entry_conditions: Normal world briefly established to create contrast
exit_conditions: Audience understands that something threatening is present, even if its nature is not yet clear
typical_variants: Unexplained phenomenon (object moved, sound heard, face at window)
Discovery of evidence of prior horror (previous victim, disturbed space)
First direct encounter with threat that protagonist cannot explain
Violation of the ordinary that cannot be rationalized
failure_modes: Anomaly is too vague to register as genuinely alarming
Anomaly is immediately explained away with too much ease
First scare is so extreme it leaves nowhere for escalation to go
signals_in_text: Protagonist's reaction signals that this is not ordinary
Tone shifts perceptibly from establishment to unease
The anomaly is specific and concrete enough to stay with the audience

nodes.label: Escalation Sequence — Threat Becomes Undeniable and Personal
nodes.definition: A mid-story sequence of scenes in which the threat escalates from anomaly to undeniable danger — crossing from the ambiguous to the certain, from peripheral to personal. Characters can no longer rationalize, flee, or hope the threat will resolve itself.
entry_conditions: Protagonist has attempted to rationalize or escape the threat
exit_conditions: Threat is undeniably real, directly targeting the protagonist, and has already caused serious harm or loss
typical_variants: Direct confrontation that cannot be denied
Loss of an ally or loved one to the threat
Revelation of the threat's full scope or nature
Protagonist trapped with the threat, escape closed off
failure_modes: Escalation is repetitive — same type of scare without raising stakes
Threat becomes personal through coincidence rather than earned narrative logic
Escalation skips steps — jumps from anomaly to climax without middle development
signals_in_text: Characters explicitly acknowledge that the threat is real and dangerous
Attempts to escape or neutralize the threat have failed
The cost of the threat is now visible and personal — not just abstract danger

nodes.label: Final Confrontation / Survival Scene — Face the Threat at Maximum Danger
nodes.definition: The climactic scene in which the protagonist faces the threat at its most dangerous, with minimum resources, maximum vulnerability, and no remaining safety net. Horror does not require the threat to be defeated — only that the confrontation occurs at the highest possible stakes.
entry_conditions: All escalation has led to this point
Protagonist's resources, allies, and options are depleted
Threat is at its most powerful or most proximate
exit_conditions: Central threat is confronted — and the narrative resolves, whether through survival, defeat, ambiguous escape, or terrible cost
typical_variants: Survival against overwhelming threat (escape, not defeat)
Sacrifice — protagonist or another pays the cost to end the threat
Pyrrhic victory — threat is ended but at devastating personal cost
Ambiguous ending — protagonist survives but threat may not be fully resolved
failure_modes: Climax is resolved through deus ex machina or outside intervention
Protagonist defeats the threat too easily, negating prior vulnerability
Resolution is emotionally unsatisfying because it has no cost
signals_in_text: Highest intensity and danger in the story
Protagonist uses knowledge or experience earned through prior horror
Resolution — even survival — carries weight and consequence

nodes.label: Sustained Dread Atmosphere
nodes.definition: Horror requires a pervasive, sustained atmosphere of dread that operates below the level of discrete scare scenes — a tonal undercurrent that makes even quiet moments feel unsafe, that invests the ordinary with menace, and that keeps the audience in a state of anxious anticipation rather than comfort.
exit_conditions: Dread is present in the majority of scenes, with moments of apparent relief functioning to deepen rather than release tension
typical_variants: Oppressive, suffocating dread (nowhere is safe)
Creeping, slow-building unease (something is wrong but not yet named)
Paranoid atmosphere (threat could be anywhere or anyone)
Existential weight (the horror has implications beyond the immediate danger)
failure_modes: Extended comfortable stretches break the horror contract
Dread exists only in action-horror set pieces
Comic relief undercuts dread without serving a narrative purpose
Tone is inconsistent — horror and non-horror registers alternate without intention
signals_in_text: Environmental and sensory details carry ominous weight
Characters' emotional states reflect sustained fear rather than episodic fright
Pacing and prose style create tension independent of plot events

nodes.label: Consequence-Free Scares — Boy Who Cried Wolf
nodes.definition: Horror fails when scares are repeated without consequence, accumulation, or cost — when the audience learns that frightening moments will resolve safely, that characters will recover without lasting damage, or that the threat will not follow through. Consequence-free scares train the audience to stop feeling fear.
entry_conditions: Scare or threat moment is attempted
exit_conditions: Every scare or threat moment carries weight, cost, or advancement of danger — nothing resets to baseline safety
typical_variants: Repeated jump scares with no lasting effect
Threat confrontations that leave protagonist unharmed each time
False alarms used as the primary scare mechanism
Character injuries or trauma that disappear between scenes
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a horror genre violation
signals_in_text: Audience stops reacting to threat moments
Characters behave as if prior scares had no cost or meaning
Threat is frequently invoked but never lands

edges.label: Promise requires a present, escalating, genuinely dangerous threat
edges.effects_on_stakes: Threat must be real and grow in danger across the story — not a static backdrop
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist is under continuously mounting pressure from a threat that is getting worse
common_alternatives: Threat escalation may be physical, psychological, or cosmological depending on subgenre
anti_patterns: Threat is mentioned but does not demonstrably escalate
Danger plateaus after the first act

edges.label: Promise requires a vulnerable protagonist outmatched by the threat
edges.effects_on_stakes: Vulnerability makes the threat's danger credible — the audience believes harm can occur
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist cannot simply overpower the threat — must survive through wit, luck, or partial knowledge
common_alternatives: Vulnerability may be physical, epistemic, social, or psychological
anti_patterns: Protagonist is too capable — threat never feels real
Easy access to tools or knowledge that neutralize the threat

edges.label: Promise requires sustained atmosphere of dread beyond discrete scares
edges.effects_on_stakes: Dread must permeate the narrative — not just punctuate it with scare moments
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must exist in a state of sustained psychological pressure, not just episodic fright
common_alternatives: Dread may be slow-burn, oppressive, uncanny, or existential depending on subgenre
anti_patterns: Dread exists only in isolated scare scenes
Comic relief or comfort stretches undercut the atmosphere

edges.label: Promise requires a threat with internal logic — rules even if unknown
edges.effects_on_stakes: Consistent threat logic makes danger feel real and learnable — even partial knowledge matters
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's attempts to understand the threat are meaningful and potentially useful
common_alternatives: Logic may be supernatural, predatory, psychological, or cosmic — but must be consistent
anti_patterns: Threat behaves randomly based on plot convenience
Rules are stated and then ignored

edges.label: Promise sets the sustained dread tone
edges.effects_on_stakes: Even non-threat scenes carry ominous weight
edges.effects_on_character: Characters exist in a world that feels fundamentally unsafe
anti_patterns: Extended safe and comfortable stretches
Tone inconsistency between horror and non-horror registers

edges.label: Escalating threat takes supernatural form
preconditions: Threat is established as supernatural in origin
edges.effects_on_stakes: Threat operates by rules that exceed physical or human limitations — cannot be defeated by ordinary means
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist must confront a reality that violates their prior understanding of the world
anti_patterns: Supernatural nature is claimed but threat behaves like a mundane physical danger

edges.label: Escalating threat takes predatory human form
preconditions: Threat is a human or near-human relentless predator
edges.effects_on_stakes: Physical danger is visceral and immediate — survival is the only goal
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist is reduced to survival mode — all resources directed toward not being killed
anti_patterns: Threat is too easily defeated
Victim behavior is implausibly stupid

edges.label: Escalating threat takes cosmically incomprehensible form
preconditions: Threat operates on a scale or in a mode beyond human comprehension
edges.effects_on_stakes: Threat's escalation erodes meaning — getting closer to the truth makes things worse, not better
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's sanity, worldview, and sense of significance are all under threat
anti_patterns: Cosmic threat is reduced to a manageable, defeatable antagonist

edges.label: Protagonist's vulnerability is primarily psychological — their own mind is the threat
preconditions: Protagonist's psychological state is a primary source of danger or threat amplification
edges.effects_on_stakes: Protagonist's perception and reality cannot be trusted — danger may be internal or external or both
edges.effects_on_character: Character is simultaneously subject and object of the horror — their mind works against them
anti_patterns: Psychological dimension resolves into a purely external threat
Ambiguity used as cop-out rather than source of dread

edges.label: Supernatural horror requires isolation — help cannot reach or believe
edges.effects_on_stakes: External help cannot address the supernatural threat even if it were available
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist must face the supernatural essentially alone — no authority or system will validate or assist
common_alternatives: Isolation may be geographic, social (no one believes), or institutional (no system recognizes supernatural threats)
anti_patterns: Protagonist has immediate access to supernatural experts who resolve the threat easily

edges.label: Supernatural horror works through the uncanny — familiar made threatening
edges.effects_on_stakes: The familiar environment becomes a source of threat — no space is safe
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist cannot trust the ordinary — every familiar thing is a potential vector for the supernatural threat
common_alternatives: Trusted person as vessel for supernatural threat
anti_patterns: Supernatural manifests only in clearly alien or marked spaces — uncanny quality absent

edges.label: Slasher horror requires isolation — escape routes must be cut off
edges.effects_on_stakes: Victims cannot simply leave — they are trapped with the threat
edges.effects_on_character: Survival requires operating within the isolated environment, not escaping it
anti_patterns: Help is readily available and protagonist simply doesn't call for implausible reasons

edges.label: Slasher threat uses darkness and concealment to operate
edges.effects_on_stakes: Threat strikes from positions where it cannot be fully anticipated or countered
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist must navigate an environment where the threat can emerge from concealment at any moment
anti_patterns: Threat operates only in clearly visible conditions — concealment absent

edges.label: Cosmic threat is concealed by its incomprehensibility — cannot be fully seen or known
edges.effects_on_stakes: Threat cannot be fully perceived or understood even when directly encountered — concealment is cognitive, not just physical
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's attempts to see and know the threat contribute to their psychological deterioration
common_alternatives: Threat known through effects rather than direct observation
Partial revelation that is worse than ignorance
anti_patterns: Cosmic threat is fully depicted and understood — scale and dread collapse

edges.label: Psychological horror weaponizes the uncanny — familiar reality becomes unreliable
edges.effects_on_stakes: Protagonist cannot trust their own environment or perception — familiar things become threatening
edges.effects_on_character: The protagonist's deteriorating perception transforms ordinary reality into a source of horror
common_alternatives: Memory as uncanny (past intrudes on present)
Relationship as uncanny (known person becomes strange)
Home as uncanny (safe space becomes threatening)
anti_patterns: Psychological threat is confined to abstract internal states with no uncanny external manifestation

edges.label: Isolation creates conditions for the first anomaly to land without rescue
edges.effects_on_stakes: Protagonist faces the first anomaly alone — there is no one to reassure them or intervene
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist must confront the anomaly without the safety net of external validation or assistance
common_alternatives: Anomaly occurs just as isolation is established
Anomaly reveals isolation to protagonist for the first time
anti_patterns: Anomaly occurs while protagonist has easy access to help who dismisses it — different dynamic

edges.label: Uncanny setting delivers the first anomaly through familiar-turned-threatening
edges.effects_on_stakes: The anomaly is more disturbing because it violates the familiar — something safe is now wrong
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's prior sense of security is specifically undermined by the anomaly in the familiar space
common_alternatives: Room that should be safe is not
anti_patterns: First anomaly occurs in an already-alien or clearly threatening space — no uncanny contrast

edges.label: Concealment shapes first anomaly — threat sensed but not fully seen
preconditions: Concealment established as operating principle of threat
edges.effects_on_stakes: First anomaly is more frightening because it is partial — the full threat remains unseen
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's imagination fills the gap — the unseen is more frightening than the fully revealed
common_alternatives: Evidence of presence without the presence itself
anti_patterns: First anomaly is a full, clear reveal of the threat — concealment principle violated

edges.label: First anomaly initiates the escalation sequence
preconditions: First anomaly established and cannot be rationalized away
edges.effects_on_stakes: Anomaly is not a one-off event — it is the first step in a building pattern of threat
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist moves from uncertainty to mounting dread as the anomaly proves to be the beginning of something worse
common_alternatives: Escalation is delayed by protagonist's denial before becoming undeniable
anti_patterns: Anomaly is isolated — never leads to escalating threat pattern
Anomaly is explained away and the story moves on without escalation

edges.label: Escalation sequence builds to the final confrontation at maximum danger
preconditions: Escalation has made threat undeniable and personal
edges.effects_on_stakes: All prior escalation has accumulated — final confrontation is at the highest possible danger level
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist has been shaped by the escalation — they bring accumulated knowledge, trauma, and desperation to the final confrontation
anti_patterns: Escalation does not lead to confrontation — story ends before the reckoning
Confrontation occurs before full escalation — stakes feel lower than they should

edges.label: Threat logic structures the escalation — rules determine how danger grows
preconditions: Threat logic established (even if not known to characters)
edges.effects_on_stakes: Escalation follows from the threat's internal logic — it is not arbitrary but has cause and direction
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's partial understanding of threat logic shapes their escalating attempts to survive or escape
common_alternatives: Escalation through violation of threat's rules
Escalation through discovery of threat's full extent
Escalation as threat's logic converges on protagonist
anti_patterns: Escalation is arbitrary — threat simply gets worse without logical cause

edges.label: Threat logic determines what confrontation can accomplish
edges.effects_on_stakes: Final confrontation's outcome is determined by the threat's rules — victory, survival, or defeat follows from those rules
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's accumulated understanding of the threat's logic is the primary resource in the final confrontation
common_alternatives: Protagonist discovers the one rule that enables survival
anti_patterns: Final confrontation ignores established threat logic — protagonist wins through a rule that was never established

edges.label: Consequence-free scares destroy the atmosphere of dread
preconditions: Scare or threat moment occurs without consequence, cost, or narrative advancement
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience stops trusting the story's threat signals — danger feels performative
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's apparent peril becomes unbelievable — the boy who cried wolf effect accumulates
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes what must be avoided to maintain the horror contract
